challenges.prompt: With no box-sizing reset applied, how wide does this element actually appear on screen?
options: 200px wide, because width sets the final rendered size
250px wide, because padding and border are added on top of the content width
230px wide, because only padding is added but the border is drawn inside
challenges.explanation: In the default content-box model, width sets only the content area. Padding and border are added outside it: 200 + 20 + 20 (padding both sides) + 5 + 5 (border both sides) = 250px. This surprise is exactly why border-box is so widely used.

challenges.prompt: Complete the reset so that width means the total rendered size, with padding and border carving into the content instead of growing the element.
challenges.explanation: box-sizing: border-box makes width and height include padding and border. This is the first thing to add to every new stylesheet so that adding padding or a border never silently grows your elements and breaks the layout.

challenges.prompt: This code has a bug — what's wrong?
options: margin-collapse is not a real CSS property; you can't disable collapse that way
padding shorthand can only take one value, not two
the padding must come after the margin declaration to work
challenges.explanation: There is no margin-collapse property. Vertical margins between block elements collapse to the larger value automatically. To avoid it, use padding on a parent container or switch to a flex or grid layout, which disables margin collapse between items.

challenges.prompt: A heading has margin-bottom: 24px and the paragraph directly below it has margin-top: 16px. Both are block elements stacked vertically. What is the actual gap between them?
options: 40px, because the two margins add together
24px, because vertical margins collapse to the larger value
16px, because the paragraph's margin wins over the heading's
challenges.explanation: Vertical margins between stacked block elements collapse: CSS takes the larger of the two (24px) as the gap rather than the sum (40px). Only top and bottom margins collapse — left and right margins always add up normally.
